result:
I've been thinking a lot about stones lately. Not gemstones or precious stones, just the ordinary ones. The kind you might kick absentmindedly on a path or pick up from a riverbed, smooth and cool in your palm.

There's a particular stone on my windowsill. It's slate grey, flecked with white, and fits perfectly in the curve of my hand when I close my fingers around it. I found it years ago on a beach in Maine, when I was walking alone, trying to untangle a problem that felt like a knot in my chest. I wasn't looking for it. My gaze was on the horizon, on the vast, indifferent Atlantic. But my foot stubbed against it, and I bent down, more out of reflex than curiosity.

It was wet then, darker, and it felt ancient. Not in a dramatic, fossil-filled way, but in a quiet, profound way. It had been tumbled by countless tides, ground against sand and other stones for who knows how many centuries. Every rough edge had been worn away. It held no inscription, no hidden crystal heart. Its entire history and purpose was simply to be a stone. To exist. To endure the water and the weather.

I took it home. It sits on my windowsill now, dry and dusty, a paperweight for nothing in particular. Sometimes, on difficult days, I pick it up. I feel its solid, undeniable weight. I think about the millennia it has witnessed. My anxiety about a missed deadline, a strained conversation, the vague, humming dread of news headlines—it all feels so frantic and temporary next to this silent piece of the earth.

We live in a world that prizes sharpness, brilliance, cutting edges. We're told to be diamonds. But I find a deeper comfort in this stone’s smoothed, unremarkable resilience. It doesn't sparkle. It doesn't have a market value. It just *is*. In its patient, unyielding presence, there is a kind of wisdom.

It reminds me that not everything needs to be a tool. Not everything needs a function or a label. Some things—and perhaps some parts of ourselves—can just be. They can be witnesses. They can hold the memory of water long after they’re dry. Their value isn't in what they produce, but in their sheer, ongoing presence.

Maybe we all need a metaphorical stone on our windowsill. Something to anchor us to a timescale larger than our own worries. A quiet, heavy truth to hold onto when the mind is a flutter of pages. A reminder that before we were ever clever, before we built cities and languages and anxieties, we came from the same stuff as this: elemental, patient, and here for the long, slow turn of the world.

So I’ll keep my stone. I’ll let it gather dust. And on the days when the noise inside and out gets too loud, I’ll close my hand around its calm, and try, for just a moment, to be as simple and as solid. To just be.
